title: Welcome to the Spaceship Titanic!
author: Ryan Holbrook
body_markdown:
Welcome to the **Spaceship Titanic**!

The dataset is a bit bigger and the features a bit more complex, but this competition has everything you love about the original [**Titanic - Machine Learning from Disaster**](https://www.kaggle.com/c/titanic) -- this time *in space*!

There's a lot to discover in this dataset. Check out some of our [free courses](https://www.kaggle.com/learn) if you'd like to brush up on the fundamentals:
- [Python](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/python) for first steps with programming
- [Intro to Machine Learning](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/intro-to-machine-learning) for learning what it's all about
- [Pandas](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/pandas) on using Python's most popular library for manipulating tabular data
- [Intermediate Machine Learning](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/intermediate-machine-learning) on essential competition skills like categorical encoding and cross-validation
- [Data Visualization](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/data-visualization) for discovering relationships in the dataset
- [Feature Engineering](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/feature-engineering) on how to improve performance by creating more informative inputs
- [Intro to Deep Learning](https://www.kaggle.com/learn/intro-to-deep-learning) to discover neural networks

Be sure to share your discoveries with a public notebook or here in the discussion forum.

Happy Kaggling!

title: This Competition has an Official Discord Channel
author: Myles O'Neill
body_markdown:
In addition to this competition forum, you can continue the discussion in our official Kaggle Discord Server here: [discord.gg/kaggle](http://discord.gg/kaggle)

The Discord is a great place to ask getting started questions, chat about the nuances of this competition, and connect with potential team mates. Learn more about Discord at our [announcement here](https://www.kaggle.com/discussions/general/429933). Here are a few things to keep in mind though:

**1. Discord Competition Channels are 'Public'**
Discord channels for specific competitions are considered 'public' spaces where you are allowed to talk about competition details (it will not count as private sharing).

**2. Discord Competition Channels are Not Monitored by Staff**
Kaggle Staff and Hosts running competitions will not monitor Discord or be available to answer questions in Discord. Always post important questions in the forums.

**3. Keep the Good Stuff on the Forums**
Please keep important questions, insights, writeups, and other valuable conversation on the Kaggle forums. Discord is intended to be a more casual space to discuss competitions and help each other, we want to keep all the best information on the forums.

**4. Remember to never privately share competition code or data**
Please remember that private sharing of competition code or data is, as always, not permitted. Code sharing must always be done publicly through the Kaggle forums/notebooks.

I hope you’ll join us to chat on Discord soon!

title: Some rules to fill NaNs
author: Vincent Debout
body_markdown:
I denote "Children", persons with age <= 12 years old.

Here are some rules I identified to fill NaNs:

- link between HomePlanet and CabinDeck:
    Earth on E, F,G
    Mars on D, E, F
    Europa on A, B, C, D, E, T
- persons in the same group all have the same CabinSide, possibly on different CabinDeck
- in groups with several CabinDeck, people with no bill are necessarily on CabinDeck:
   Earth on G
   Europa on B
   Mars on E or F
- for Europa groups with several CabinDeck, people with no bill are on deck B
- CabinNum are in increasing order of PassengerIdGroup for each (CabinDeck, CabinSide)
  => This is very useful but need a complex algorithm. All Cabins are occupied ! 
- People in a group have the same HomePlanet
- Surnames are found in a unique HomePlanet
- Children have no bill
- CryoSleep have no bill
- People not Children and not CryoSleep with no bill all have Destination TRAPPIST-1e
- link between HomePlanet, VIP (also Age and CryoSleep):
   Earth no VIP
   Mars VIP have Age >= 18 and no CryoSleep and never goes to "55 Cancri e"
   Europa VIP have Age >= 25
- Europa with Age < 25 never goes to "PSO J318.5-22"
- Mars children are all transported

Approximative rules (with some exceptions):
- Firstnames are found in a unique HomePlanet
- ToTal bill has minimum per HomePlanet and CabinDeck (Earth is around 300, Mars 400 and Europa 1000), be careful when filling with median


Please let me know if you identify additionnal rules

title: Looking for a Team Thread
author: Ashley Chow
body_markdown: Use this thread to find a teammate if you're interested in finding others to work with!

title: Feature engineering results
author: Samuel Cortinhas
body_markdown:
Here is a summary of the **feature engineering** I have tried so far and the results I got on the test set. The baseline I used can be found in my notebook [here](https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/spaceship-titanic-a-complete-guide). All I did was add/drop 1 feature at a time and see how the final score changed.

* drop **VIP** feature - 0.02% increase
* create an **expenditure** feature that sums the total money spent across the 5 amenities - 0.5% increase
* create expenditure feature and drop the 5 amenities (**RoomService**, ...,**VRDeck**) - 4.7% decrease
* create a **cabin deck** feature (A,B,...,T) - 0.35% increase
* create a **cabin side** feature (P/S) - 0.9% increase 
* create a **group size** feature from the PassengerId column - 0.1% increase
* create a **no spending** binary feature that is 1 if no money was spent and 0 otherwise - 0.2% increase
* Create a **solo** feature that tracks if passenger was travelling on their own (from PassengerId) - 0.2% increase
* Create **cabin region** features according to chunks of 300 passengers (see [here](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/316379) for details) - 0.2% increase
* create a **family size** feature from last name - 0.0% change
* create binary **age** features for 0-18 yo, 18-25 yo, 25+ yo - 0.02% increase
* same as above but drop the **age** column - 0.4% drop

**Conclusions:**
* create total expenditure, cabin deck and cabin side features - these are very helpful
* group size, solo, no_spending and cabin regions also help a bit

Do you agree? What feature engineering have you tried and did it improve your score?

title: New to Machine Learning or Kaggle?
author: Ashley Chow
body_markdown:
New to machine learning and data science? No question is too basic or too simple. Feel free to start your own thread, or use this thread as a place to post any first-timer clarifying questions for the Kaggle community to help you with!

If you would consider yourself a beginner but don't know where to get started, let other Kagglers help you take your first steps here!

New to Kaggle? Take a look at a few videos our very own Dr. Rachael Tatman has put together to learn a bit more about [site etiquette](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aIus8si_Et0), [Kaggle lingo](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sEJHyuWKd-s), and [how to enter a competition using Kaggle Notebooks](https://www.youtube.com/watch?&v=GJBOMWpLpTQ).

**Remember:** Kaggle is for everyone. Whether you're teaming up or sharing tips in the competition forum, we expect everyone to follow our [Kaggle community guidelines](https://www.kaggle.com/community-guidelines).

title: How to Choose OneHotEncoding between LabelEncoding? I'll Answer!! (EDA Tip)
author: Wongi Park
body_markdown:
We apply One-Hot Encoding when:
1. The Categorical feature is not ordinal
2. The number of categorical features is less so one-hot encoding can be effectively applied

We apply Label Encoding when:

1. The categorical feature is ordinal (like Primary school, high school)
2. The number of categories is quite large as one-hot encoding can lead to high memory consumption
<hr>

One-Hot-Encoding Feature
- HomePlanet
- CryoSleep
- Cabin (Side)
- Destination
- VIP

Label Encoding Feature
- Cabin(Deck)
- Name    (cause.2)   (many nuinque values)

<hr>
We can think about new Feature about Total bill!

Total Bill = 'RoomService' +  'FoodCourt' + 'ShoppingMall '+ 'Spa' + 'VRDeck'
* We need to deal with NaN values Before adding them.
(NaN values replace 0 beacuse,  It's most common in data.)

If you helpful, Don't forget vote!
Model tip : https://www.kaggle.com/c/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309535

Code : https://www.kaggle.com/kalelpark/spaceship-titanic-solutions-for-beginner
(Code will be Update!)

title: Starter materials
author: Ravi Ramakrishnan
body_markdown:
Hello all,

Wishing you the best for the challenge! The assignment appears to be a binary classifier, from the target, we infer that the classes are fairly balanced. 

Hope the below starter materials are useful to quickly onboard-

**Column descriptions**

1. Booking_ID: unique identifier of each booking
2. No of adults: Number of adults
3. No of children: Number of Children
4. noofweekend_nights: Number of weekend nights (Saturday or Sunday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel
5. noofweek_nights: Number of week nights (Monday to Friday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel
6. typeofmeal_plan: Type of meal plan booked by the customer:
7. requiredcarparking_space: Does the customer require a car parking space? (0 - No, 1- Yes)
8. roomtypereserved: Type of room reserved by the customer. The values are ciphered (encoded) by INN Hotels.
9. lead_time: Number of days between the date of booking and the arrival date
10. arrival_year: Year of arrival date
11. arrival_month: Month of arrival date
12. arrival_date: Date of the month
13. Market segment type: Market segment designation.
14. repeated_guest: Is the customer a repeated guest? (0 - No, 1- Yes)
15. noofprevious_cancellations: Number of previous bookings that were canceled by the customer prior to the current booking
16. noofpreviousbookingsnot_canceled: Number of previous bookings not canceled by the customer prior to the current booking
17. avgpriceper_room: Average price per day of the reservation; prices of the rooms are dynamic. (in euros)
18. noofspecial_requests: Total number of special requests made by the customer (e.g. high floor, view from the room, etc)
19. **booking_status: Flag indicating if the booking was canceled or not** -- target

**Original data link**-

https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/gauravduttakiit/reservation-cancellation-prediction

**General information on the competition metric- AUC/ GINI curve**

1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gini_coefficient -- wikipedia article
2. https://developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course/classification/roc-and-auc -- short and terse article on the measure
3. https://towardsdatascience.com/understanding-auc-roc-curve-68b2303cc9c5 -- well curated GINI related article
4. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OAl6eAyP-yo -- introductory YouTube video for the GINI measure
5. https://acutecaretesting.org/en/articles/roc-curves-what-are-they-and-how-are-they-used -- another great article on the GINI measure

**Recent TPS binary classifiers**

My below discussion post highlights a few binary classifiers from the past year's edition of the playground series and some useful resources for the same- 
https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/playground-series-s3e2/discussion/377171

**Playground Season 3 binary classifier resources**

The below kernels could be of use, based on recent season 3 problems with the same metric too-

**Episode2**
1. https://www.kaggle.com/code/dmitryuarov/ps-s3e2-catboost-lasso-nn-fiasco -- popular kernel from Playground Season3- Episode2
2. https://www.kaggle.com/code/tilii7/modeling-stroke-dataset-with-lasso-regression -- uses a LASSO regression to predict the target, it could be an alternative approach to the problem too
3. https://www.kaggle.com/code/sergiosaharovskiy/ps-s3e2-2023-eda-and-base-pytorch-model -- uses PyTorch to great effect and is a very beginner friendly kernel

**Episode3**
4. https://www.kaggle.com/code/phongnguyen1/s3e3-from-eda-to-final-submission -- comprehensive kernel highlighting an effective process flow from EDA-submission to great effect
5. https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/ps-s3e3-hill-climbing-like-a-gm -- great kernel explaining **hill climbing** ensemble strategy to blend predictions
6. https://www.kaggle.com/code/alexandershumilin/ps-s3-e3-ensemble-model-woe-encoding-optuna -- great kernel, explaining the usage of various model approaches with tuning using optuna also

**General kernels for binary classification**

1. https://www.kaggle.com/code/ravi20076/titanic-eda-model-custompipelines -- titanic binary classifier with EDA and custom pipelines with an array of model options
2. https://www.kaggle.com/code/startupsci/titanic-data-science-solutions/comments -- one of the best Titanic classifier tutorials on Kaggle with beginner friendly code and lots of comments and explanations
3. https://www.kaggle.com/code/prashant111/naive-bayes-classifier-in-python -- excellent beginner friendly notebook highlighting the naive bayes method on an approachable dataset
4. https://www.kaggle.com/code/prashant111/decision-tree-classifier-tutorial -- excellent Decision Tree tutorial notebook with lots of explanations, comments and fantastic visuals and tree diagrams
5. https://www.kaggle.com/code/willkoehrsen/start-here-a-gentle-introduction -- excellent notebook on an approachable dataset and use-case with good quality EDA, great visuals and a well drafted baseline logistic model, perfect starter for a competition
6. https://www.kaggle.com/code/devsubhash/tps-august-eda-failure-prediction -- excellent EDA and classical ML based approach on a very beginner friendly dataset
7. https://www.kaggle.com/code/ambrosm/tpsaug22-eda-which-makes-sense -- excellent EDA oriented notebook for an approachable use-case and basic ML models, apt for a beginner to start and onboard onto ML assignments
8. https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/spaceship-titanic-a-complete-guide -- great visuals, good EDA and simple models on an approachable starter competition use-case. This is an ideal starter notebook for the competition and is highly beginner friendly

Best regards and happy learning!

title: Tip - Don't forget about the Cabin! 📈
author: Alex Teboul
body_markdown:
For a slight **increase in accuracy**, don't drop the **Cabin** column entirely from your modeling.

**For example:**
```
# split column and add new columns to df
train[['Deck', 'Num', 'Side']] = train['Cabin'].str.split('/', expand=True)
# display the dataframe
train.head()
```

[![Screen-Shot-2022-02-24-at-4-07-00-PM.png](https://i.postimg.cc/BQ8rqMQH/Screen-Shot-2022-02-24-at-4-07-00-PM.png)](https://postimg.cc/RNBsd1tZ)

## [Notebook Link](https://www.kaggle.com/alexteboul/space-titanic-eda-models-lb-0-799l)

title: [Suggestion] - Kindly consider organizing few playground episodes for beginners/ new accounts only
author: Ravi Ramakrishnan
body_markdown:
Hello Kaggle team,

I am sure we all concur that the Playground Series competitions are developed to provide a conduit to beginners to onboard into Kaggle and step up from starter assignments like the Titanic challenge and Spaceship Titanic assignment and then eventually to attempt featured and research assignments. The image from an older playground episode corroborates this - 

![](https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/kaggle-forum-message-attachments/o/inbox%2F8273630%2F53cd6fc27efc08115687045e8b7f0528%2FPlayground.png?generation=1739606695039387&alt=media)

Considering this, I may suggest to Kaggle to consider organizing a few episodes in the year **only for beginners**, the real consumer of the series. Perhaps the following participant base may be considered - 
1. Include people with new Kaggle accounts in the past 6 months 
2. Include people with 1-3 attempts at the playground series, please consider excluding participants winning past episodes to direct the said episodes for newcomers  
3. Exclude Masters and Grandmasters across all tiers in these episodes
4. Include participants in the age group of 15-20 / students if data is available 
5. Include anyone else who identifies himself/ herself as a beginner as per Kaggle's discretion 

Perhaps organizing a CPU-only code competition for this participant group will also place them at the same level in terms of resources and orient them effectively into code submissions in other competitions. Perhaps, this will also provide a fresh learning perspective for others like me with a long experience in the series and on Kaggle in general. 

Regards.

title: A Summary of all top discussion posts
author: The Devastator
body_markdown:
## A Summary of all top discussion posts
Below is a summary of all the top discussion posts of this competition so far. 
Made by myself. 
_____


[Make the best use of "Deck" feature](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/310238) 
by [mostafaalaa123](mostafaalaa123)

- From the comments:
- I think the idea is to create a new feature that is 1 if the deck is safe and 0 if it is dangerous.

["Gender" didn't help me](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/310239) 
by [mostafaalaa123](mostafaalaa123)

- Adding gender as a feature didn't help the author's model.

[Spaceship Titanic Compare BaseModels EveryBody Get Insight!](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309535)
by [kalelpark](kalelpark)

The author used pycaret to compere many models to try and find the best one. These were the results:

![](https://i.ibb.co/26xKhJd/2022-02-24-094651.jpg)

**Top 5 Models**
- LightGBM - 79.1%
- GradientBoostingClassifier - 79.6%
- AdaBoostClassifier - 79.4%
- RandomForest - 79.2%
- CatBoost - 79.6%

[Question - How to deal with this kind of distribution ?](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/310009) 
by [kartik2khandelwal](kartik2khandelwal)

- The author was looking at the distribution of some of the features and asked for guidance about dealing with such a distribution.

**Options proposed**
- **Remove Outliers** - but this will result in less data
- **Perform some transformation technique** - log, sqrt, boxcox and so on…
- **Make it a categorical variable** by putting all the outliers as 0 and other data as 1 - may leads to less information.

**From the comments**

- **Remove Outliers** - I would advise not to do so. Usually, if we remove outliers it is because we make the hypothesis that the event is due to some kind of artifact (a wrong record). But here, people paying a lot is not a wrong record and it could actually be valuable data.
- **Perform some transformation** - Every variable is not meant to be normally distributed. I think we can even expect this variable to be distributed as shown as we would expect only a few amounts of people to be able to spend that much. If you use log transform, for example, you will only compress your data and lose most of the value it holds. Except if you need to approximate a normal distribution for analysis purposes, or because you need to satisfy the normal distribution hypothesis for one of your processing steps, I would suggest not to.
- **Make it a categorical variable** - As discussed previously, we can't really talk of outliers here. An outlier is a record that seems abnormal based on the hypothesis of the distribution it follows. So, I would suggest not applying this categorical transformation either.

- **Perform some transformation** One could use a non-parametric estimator, such as decision trees or developments thereof, such as RandomForest, XGBoost, or HistGradientBoostingClassifier etc. thus obviating the need to transform the features to become Gaussian.
- **Make it a categorical variable** Rather than actually replacing the feature as a categorical variable, create a new additional feature as a categorical variable, perhaps with something like

- `df['food_spending'] = np.where(df['FoodCourt'] > 1000, 'yes', 'no')`


[[Exploratory Data Analysis] High cardinality & High correlation](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309513) 
by [rishirajacharya](rishirajacharya)

The author is sharing some findings of EDA:

- `PassengerId` has a high cardinality: 8693 distinct values
- `Cabin` has a high cardinality: 6560 distinct values
- `Name` has a high cardinality: 8473 distinct values
- `FoodCourt` is highly correlated with VRDeck`
- `CryoSleep` is highly correlated with `Transported`
- `HomePlanet` is highly correlated with `Destination`

[Tip - Don't forget about the Cabin! 📈](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309693)
by [alexteboul](alexteboul)

- From the post: "For a slight **increase in accuracy**, don't drop the `Cabin` column entirely from your modeling."

**For Example**
```python
# split column and add new columns to df
train[['Deck', 'Num', 'Side']] = train['Cabin'].str.split('/', expand=True)
# display the dataframe
train.head()
```
- [notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/alexteboul/space-titanic-eda-models-lb-0-799l)

[How to Choose OneHotEncoding between LabelEncoding? I'll Answer!! (EDA Tip)](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309803) 
by [kalelpark](kalelpark)

- From the post:

**We apply One-Hot Encoding when:**

- The Categorical feature is not ordinal
- The number of categorical features is less so one-hot encoding can be effectively applied

**We apply Label Encoding when:**
- The categorical feature is ordinal (like Primary school, high school)
- The number of categories is quite large as one-hot encoding can lead to high memory consumption

The author also shared a public [notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/kalelpark/spaceship-titanic-solutions-for-beginner)

[Might be a good way to fill some missing  value?](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/310574) 
by [gargantuar](gargantuar)

- Found that for the passenger when the first part of the PassengerId is same ,the Homeplanet ,Cabin are also same.
- So, we can fill some missing value of these two values(about 50 in total 200),that might be able to improve the data quality.

[Adding GENDER Feature based on NAME column](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309664) 
by [sardorabdirayimov](sardorabdirayimov)

- The notebook uses an [external web page](https://www.gpeters.com/names/baby-names.php) to identify gender based on the name
- Also: `Name` column in the dataset definitely helps to identify `gender`, `family`, `nationality` , `personality`


[📌 EDA📈 + FE🔥 + Comparison⚖️ + Pseudo🎭](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309626) 
by [hasanbasriakcay](hasanbasriakcay)

- **Shared multiple notebooks:**
- [EDA](https://www.kaggle.com/hasanbasriakcay/spaceship-titanic-eda-fe-baseline)
- [Model Comparison](https://www.kaggle.com/hasanbasriakcay/spaceship-titanic-pycaret-model-comparisons)
- [Pseudo Labeling](https://www.kaggle.com/hasanbasriakcay/spaceship-titanic-pseudo-labels-stacking)

[Cabin Feature is more IMPORTANT than you believe](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/319152)
by [javigallego](javigallego)

- Sharing a code snippet for splitting the Cabin feature into 3 new features.
```python
def split_Cabin(df):
    df['Deck'] = df['Cabin'].str.split("/", n=2, expand=True)[0]
    df['Number'] = df['Cabin'].str.split("/", n=2, expand=True)[1]
    df['Side'] = df['Cabin'].str.split("/", n=2, expand=True)[2]
    df.pop('Cabin')
    return df
```

[Feature engineering results](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/316768)
by [samuelcortinhas](samuelcortinhas)

**Sharing the results of Feature Engineering:**
- drop VIP feature - 0.02% increase
- create an expenditure feature that sums the total money spent across the 5 amenities - 0.5% increase
- create expenditure feature and drop the 5 amenities (RoomService, …,VRDeck) - 4.7% decrease
- create a cabin deck feature (A,B,…,T) - 0.35% increase
- create a cabin side feature (P/S) - 0.9% increase
- create a group size feature from the PassengerId column - 0.1% increase
- create a no spending binary feature that is 1 if no money was spent and 0 otherwise - 0.2% increase
- Create a solo feature that tracks if passenger was travelling on their own (from PassengerId) - 0.2% increase
- Create cabin region features according to chunks of 300 passengers (see here for details) - 0.2% increase-
- create a family size feature from last name - 0.0% change
- create binary age features for 0-18 yo, 18-25 yo, 25+ yo - 0.02% increase
- same as above but drop the age column - 0.4% drop

**Conclusions:**
- create total expenditure, cabin deck and cabin side features - these are very helpful
- group size, solo, no_spending and cabin regions also help a bit

[full notebook here](https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/spaceship-titanic-a-complete-guide)

by [rocklen](rocklen)

[Random Forest Perform significantly better in some cases than XGBoost](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/314044)
by [shivamnegi1993](shivamnegi1993)

- Random Forest is performing better than XGBoost in some cases.
- Check cases on the original thread.

[Some rules to fill NaNs](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/315987) 
by [vdebout](vdebout)

- The author shared some hardcoded rules to fill NaNs.

- link between HomePlanet and CabinDeck:
    Earth on E, F,G
    Mars on D, E, F
    Europa on A, B, C, D, E, T
- persons in the same group all have the same CabinSide, possibly on different CabinDeck
    in groups with several CabinDeck, people with no bill are necessarily on CabinDeck:
    Earth on G
    Europa on B
    Mars on E or F
- for Europa groups with several CabinDeck, people with no bill are on deck B
- CabinNum are in increasing order of PassengerIdGroup for each (CabinDeck, CabinSide) => This is very useful but need a complex algorithm. All Cabins are occupied !
- People in a group have the same HomePlanet
- Surnames are found in a unique HomePlanet
- Children have no bill
- CryoSleep have no bill
- People not Children and not CryoSleep with no bill all have Destination TRAPPIST-1e
- link between HomePlanet, VIP (also Age and CryoSleep):
    Earth no VIP
    Mars VIP have Age >= 18 and no CryoSleep and never goes to "55 Cancri e"
    Europa VIP have Age >= 25
    Europa with Age < 25 never goes to "PSO J318.5-22"
    Mars children are all transported

**Approximative rules (with some exceptions):**
- Firstnames are found in a unique HomePlanet
- ToTal bill has minimum per HomePlanet and CabinDeck (Earth is around 300, Mars 400 and Europa 1000), be careful when filling with median


[Tip. Increase your score!](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/312173) 
by [michael6141](michael6141)

- Sharing a tip to increase your score: "Try to average received probabilities on each validation split (list(probabilities)/len(probabilities)), predict_proba may help you. Averaging the model is a powerful tool to get a high validation score."

[[Exploratory Data Analysis] Missing Values](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/309514)
by [rishirajacharya](rishirajacharya)

- Age has 179 (2.1%) missing values
- Destination has 182 (2.1%) missing values
- FoodCourt has 183 (2.1%) missing values
- HomePlanet has 201 (2.3%) missing values
- Spa has 183 (2.1%) missing values
- VIP has 203 (2.3%) missing values
- RoomService has 181 (2.1%) missing values
- VRDeck has 188 (2.2%) missing values
- Name has 200 (2.3%) missing values
- From the post:
- Cabin has 199 (2.3%) missing values
- ShoppingMall has 208 (2.4%) missing values
- CryoSleep has 217 (2.5%) missing values

- Sharing a [public notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/rishirajacharya/spaceship-titanic-detailed) about the missing values in the dataset.

[[Spaceship Titanic] TensorFlow Extended - Towards ML Engineering ](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic/discussion/328882)
by [usharengaraju](usharengaraju)

- Sharing in depth analysis of the dataset using TensorFlow.
- Sharing a [public notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/harshjain123/time-series-arima-sarima) about the following time series concepts:

title: Rolling Leaderboards
author: Ashley Chow
body_markdown:
This competition is a *Getting Started* competition, meaning that it will not have a set deadline. As the competition grows, we want to ensure it does not outgrow its own leaderboard!

To keep with the spirit of getting-started competitions, we will implement a two month rolling window on submissions. Once a submission is more than two months old, it will be invalidated and no longer count towards the leaderboard. If your team has no submissions in the previous two months, the team will also drop from the leaderboard. This will keep the leaderboard at a manageable size, freshen it up, and prevent newcomers from getting lost in a sea of abandoned scores. 

To quote @wcukierski: "Consider this a change to be more like a college class: your professor decides the curve based on how your classmates score, not based on all exam scores since 1986."

*"I worked so hard to get that score! What happened to it!?!"*

Getting started competitions have always been for educational purposes. While the score goes away, you still get to keep what you learned. (…or you can always re-submit!) Besides, with a public ground truth and never-ending timeline, it was never the case that getting-started leaderboards were an honest representation of merit.

Have fun and happy modeling!

title: Interesting ways to fill NULL Values - Improving accuracy by ~2%
author: Praveen Kumar
body_markdown:
During my data analysis phase, I discovered the following, more practical ways to fulfill Null values, at least to some specific features.

**HomePlanet:** Instead of directly it to be earth, you and feature engineer null values based on the following 2 scenarios.
i) People who are travelling as a group, are more likely to come from the same home planet. Thus, if you find missing values within the same group, it's better to fill them based on group values. 
ii) People who are not traveling in a group, are not 100% to come from Earth. So, instead, you can look at the probability distribution of different home planets, and fill the remaining null values based on the normal distribution. This will additionally maintain consistency in data rather than creating bias.
**CryoSleep, Destination & VIP can be filled in a similar manner**

 
Additionally, people have combined 'RoomService, FoodCourt, shopping mall, Spa, VRDeck' into a single column by adding each value. However, 'RoomService & FoodCourt' are essentials for a living but not "shopping mall, Spa & VRDeck". So, these features can be split into 2 features. People who spend more on living essentials seem to be richer, thus having higher status among all the people onboard. 


Bringing practical intuition into machine learning could give you better results.
Hope this helps.

title: predict Vs predict_proba methods
author: Carl McBride Ellis
body_markdown:
### `predict()` Vs `predict_proba()`
Both the `train.csv` and the `test.csv` datasets in this competition are the very same as those in the standard [Spaceship Titanic](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic) competition so EDA work is pretty much transferable. However, a significant difference is the `submission.csv`. In both the *Titanic* and the *Spaceship Titanic* one is using the accuracy score, which is based on binary predictions hence the use of the [**`predict`** method](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/glossary.html#term-predict). In this competition, using the Brier score, we would do better using the [**`predict_proba`** method](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/glossary.html#term-predict_proba) which returns the confidence in a prediction (I am hesitant to say the actual probability associated with a prediction, perhaps more like a score). Note that, as well as many of the scikit-learn classifiers, [XGBoost](https://xgboost.readthedocs.io/en/stable/python/python_api.html#xgboost.XGBClassifier.predict_proba), [LightGBM](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pythonapi/lightgbm.LGBMClassifier.html#lightgbm.LGBMClassifier.predict_proba) and [CatBoost](https://catboost.ai/en/docs/concepts/python-reference_catboostclassifier_predict_proba) also provide `predict_proba` methods. Usage example: for "probabilities" ```python predictions = classifier.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1] ``` rather than the following for classes ```python predictions = classifier.predict(X_test) ``` I hope this helps! All the best, carl

title: Tip. Increase your score!
author: Manhow
body_markdown: Everybody looking for an idea on how to increase your score I dedicate this topic. Being a novice I found a method that allows me to stay in top 10 (10.03.2022). Try to average received probabilities on each validation split (list(probabilities)/len(probabilities)), predict_proba may help you. Averaging the model is a powerful tool to get a high validation score.💯🙂

title: "Gender" didn't help me
author: Mostafa Alaa
body_markdown: I thought I will be the highest score by adding this by extracting first name and mapping to get the gender, but the first surprise was half of the names aren't on the datasets, I come up with (believe or not I used about 7 or 8), so I tried to predict the rest of the genders by the rest of the features then I put this feature ("is_female") to advance my model performance, and I didn't get my model advanced.....short sad story😂

title: Cabin number chunking
author: Samuel Cortinhas
body_markdown:
I've been analyzing the cabin feature today and I came across this insight. It seems that the cabin number is chunked into groups of 300 cabins, and within each group the target distribution is a little bit different.

[![Cabin-number-distribution.png](https://i.postimg.cc/NjpRsTXT/Cabin-number-distribution.png)](https://postimg.cc/CRnRJRmL)

This means we can compress this information by creating a new feature that tracks which chunk the passenger is in. I implemented this in my notebook, you can see here for details. [https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/spaceship-titanic-a-complete-guide](https://www.kaggle.com/code/samuelcortinhas/spaceship-titanic-a-complete-guide)

title: [Spaceship Titanic] TensorFlow Extended - Towards ML Engineering
author: Tensor Girl
body_markdown:
TensorFlow Extended (TFX) is used to create machine learning pipelines. It is open-source and is used by many large companies to put their machine learning model into production.

![im](https://i.imgur.com/Npjr3NK.png)


**Pipeline :**
A TFX pipeline is used to implement an ML pipeline which remains intact for the entire lifetime of the product. They are built using tfx components. 

![](https://i.imgur.com/z3mMdts.png)

**Metadata store**-

It is a single place to manage all the ML metadata about experiments, artifacts, models, and pipelines.
A store consists of - 
Definitions of artifacts and their properties
Execution records of components
Lineage tracking across all executions

![](https://i.imgur.com/DwDEvHq.png)

**TFX Orchestrator**-
An Orchestrator is a system where you can execute pipeline runs. Apache airflow, kubeflow are used as orchestrators. Dagrunner is used to refer to an implementation that supports orchestrator.

**Components**-
A component is used to implement a part of the ML pipeline. ML pipeline consists of many such components. Components are composed of:

component specification- Defines the component's input, output and required parameters.
executor- Implements the code in that component
component interface- Contains component specification and executor
 
Explaining each component here-

**ExampleGen**- This component ingests data into the pipelines. It takes external files (in formats such as CSV, TFRecord, BigQuery etc),partitions and shuffles it, to generate the Examples file. 

![](https://i.imgur.com/hrb82Ev.png)

**StatisticsGen**-
It generates features statistics over Example data and releases data statistics for other pipeline components.

![](https://i.imgur.com/Ym9pOZd.png)

**SchemaGen**
Schema provides a description of input data. SchemaGen automatically generates a schema from the training data and provides detail about the features, their allowed values and data types for feature values.

![](https://i.imgur.com/MySRzxs.png)

**ExampleValidator**
It finds anomalies in training and serving data by comparing data statistics computed by the StatisticsGen pipeline component against a schema. For example: 

![](https://i.imgur.com/FMi2ZWn.png)

**Transform**
It performs feature engineering on Example data and releases both SavedModel as well as statistics on both pre-transform and post-transform data.

![](https://i.imgur.com/R1OYqkV.png)

**Trainer**
Trainer is used to train models using tensorflow.
It takes Examples data, logic for training the data and protobuf definition for trainargs and eval argos. It releases a model for inference and maybe another one for evaluation

![](https://i.imgur.com/u6NqxPJ.png)

**Evaluator**
It performs analysis on the training results for the models to access overall model quality and track performance over time. It validates if the model can be pushed for production.
If the new model's metrics meet the baseline model requirements, the model is said to be "blessed", and is pushed to production.

![](https://i.imgur.com/9dXiq4y.png)

**Pusher**
IT is used to push a validated model to a deployment. 
 
The Pusher component pushes a validated model to a deployment target during model training . Pusher decides to push or not based on the blessings from the other components.
Evaluator blesses the model if it is "good enough" to be pushed to production.
InfraValidator blesses the model if the model is mechanically servable in a production environment.
A Pusher component consumes a trained model in SavedModel format, and outputs  the same SavedModel, along with versioning metadata.
 
 ![](https://i.imgur.com/rqyuCgC.png)·        
 

The ExampleGen TFX Pipeline component ingests data into TFX pipelines by consuming external data sources such as CSV, TFRecord, Avro, Parquet and BigQuery to generate Examples( tf.Example records, tf.SequenceExample records, or proto format) which will be read by other TFX components. 
ExampleGen and Other Components
ExampleGen provides data to components that make use of the TensorFlow Data Validation library, such as SchemaGen, StatisticsGen, and Example Validator. It also provides data to Transform, which makes use of the TensorFlow Transform library, and ultimately to deployment targets during inference.
 



**Kindly restart the colab runtime after TFX installation to run without errors**

Below is tutorial series on TensorFlow Extended using SpaceShip Titanic Dataset

TFX Starter Pipeline

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1KCLbxVItQpAITemVrjPs64yX3-RS1J--?usp=sharing


TFX Interactive :

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1kj65CT3d4ninQhJT_NXj5yI5t6vuZ5fa?usp=sharing

TFMA

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1tTG80-wgWl5nnCc5NPqeGTjqdhAypMDB?usp=sharing

title: Rank 8 approach - simple ML models and Optuna ensemble
author: Ravi Ramakrishnan
body_markdown:
Hello all,

My 8th place kernels are public and are available at the below links-
1. https://www.kaggle.com/code/ravi20076/spaceshiptitanic-models/notebook
2. https://www.kaggle.com/code/ravi20076/spaceshiptitanic-blending

# Approach

## Feature engineering
Thanks to @arunklenin for the FE notebook from the other Spaceship Titanic competition. Kernel is [here](https://www.kaggle.com/code/arunklenin/space-titanic-eda-advanced-feature-engineering)

## Model training
1. I used a stratified 10-fold CV scheme 
2. I resorted to a gamut of gbdt models including Catboost, LightGBM and XgBoost along with scikit-learn models like Random Forest and Histogram Gradient Boosting Classifier for the pipeline

## Blending 
I used Optuna to blend the results of the individual models

## Areas of improvement 
1. I could have used AutoML tools 
2. I could have experimented with NNs
3. Better blending strategies may have created a better result 

# Concluding remarks 
Thanks to @carlmcbrideellis for the competition.
Happy learning and best regards!

title: Welcome to the Spaceship Titanic ...in all probability!
author: Carl McBride Ellis
body_markdown:
Welcome to the **Spaceship Titanic ...in all probability!**

This competition is a probabilistic variant of the famous kaggle [Spaceship Titanic](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/spaceship-titanic) competition, it uses the same data but instead of the  classification accuracy the submissions are evaluated based on the [Brier score](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brier_score), the smaller the Brier score the better. To do well in this competition it is convenient to create a **well calibrated classifier**. For more on calibration see the scikit-learn page on [probability calibration](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/calibration.html), as well as taking a look at these suggested papers:
* Alexandru Niculescu-Mizil and Rich Caruana ["*Predicting Good Probabilities With Supervised Learning*"](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/~alexn/papers/calibration.icml05.crc.rev3.pdf)
* Telmo Silva Filho, *et al.* ["*Classifier calibration: a survey on how to assess and improve predicted class probabilities*"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2112.10327.pdf)
* Vladimir Vovk, Ivan Petej, and Valentina Fedorova ["*Large-scale probabilistic prediction with
and without validity guarantees*"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1511.00213.pdf)
* Vladimir Vovk, and Ivan Petej ["*Venn-Abers Predictors*"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1211.0025.pdf)

All the best and good luck!
carl
